Introduction to SimKriging Package

In this document, we illustrate the R package SimKriging (https://github.com/tianyi-pan/SimKriging) for simulation study of relative prediction efficiency of kriging to co-kriging, which is a replication the simulation study in Zhang and Cai (2015). The R script and results can be found in the repos.

## Some dependencies
# install.packages(c('Rcpp', 'RcppEigen', 'RcppArmadillo'))
# install.packages('devtools')

## Install Package
devtools::install_github('tianyi-pan/SimKriging')
## load package
library(SimKriging)
#> Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'RcppEigen':
#>   method               from         
#>   predict.fastLm       RcppArmadillo
#>   print.fastLm         RcppArmadillo
#>   summary.fastLm       RcppArmadillo
#>   print.summary.fastLm RcppArmadillo

Description of SimKriging Package

We seek to address the open question proposed in the assigned paper (Genton and Kleiber 2015), “for the proposes of co-kriging, in what situations are the use of nontrivial cross-covariances beneficial”, under the specific case:

Following Genton and Kleiber (2015), we consider a bivariate Gaussian process with mean 0 and the exponential cross-covariance functions in a one-dimension spatial coordinate.

Let \(\boldsymbol{Y}(\boldsymbol{s}) = [Y_1(\boldsymbol{s}),Y_2(\boldsymbol{s})]^\mathrm{T}\) be a stationary bivariate Gaussian process. The spatial process \(Y_1(\boldsymbol{s})\) that we aim to predict is called the primary process,and then \(Y_2(\boldsymbol{s})\) is called the auxiliary process.

The exponential cross-covariance function is specified as follows, \[ C_{ij}(\boldsymbol{s}_1, \boldsymbol{s}_2) = \sigma_{ij}^2 \exp (-\alpha ||\boldsymbol{h}||), \quad i, j = 1, 2, \] where \(\boldsymbol{h} = \boldsymbol{s}_1-\boldsymbol{s}_2\) and \(||\cdot||\) denotes the Euclidean norm. \(C_{ij}(\cdot,\cdot)\) is the cross-covariance function define in Genton and Kleiber (2015), and \(\alpha > 0\) is the scale parameter Gneiting et al. (2010). The marginal covariances \(\sigma_{12}^2\) and \(\sigma_{21}^2\) can be reparametrized as \(r \sigma_{11} \sigma_{22}\), where \(r\) is the correlation coefficient of \(Y_1(s)\) and \(Y_2(s)\) Genton and Kleiber (2015).

The data are sampled by a certain scheme as in Zhang and Cai (2015).

Our research question is:

Under the specific case, how do the scale parameter \(\alpha\), correlation coefficient \(r\) and sample size \(n\) affect the relative prediction efficiency of kriging to co-kriging, where the prediction efficiency is measured by mean squared prediction error?

The package SimKriging was designed to investigate the results question through simulation studies.

The performance measure is selected as the Relative Prediction Efficiency (RPE), \[ \text{RPE} = \frac{\mathrm{E}(Y_1(0) - \widehat{Y}_{1,\text{co-kriging}}(0))^2}{\mathrm{E}(Y_1(0) - \widehat{Y}_{1,\text{kriging}}(0))^2}, \] where \(\mathrm{E}(Y_1(0) - \widehat{Y}_{1,\text{co-kriging}}(0))^2\) and \(\mathrm{E}(Y_1(0) - \widehat{Y}_{1,\text{kriging}}(0))^2\) are MSPE’s of co-kriging and kriging predictors for \(Y_1(0)\).

In this simulationstudy package, we provide functions for running the simulation, and summarizing results in figures and tables. We will introduce the functions in the package, and then discuss the programming checking.

An example for SimKriging Package

We will describe how to use the package through an example with the following choice of levels as in the final report and Zhang and Cai (2015):

  • Correlation coefficient \(r\): 0.2, 0.5
  • Scale parameter \(\alpha\): 2, 4, 8
  • Sample size \(n\): 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 100

Main function

The main function of the package is do_sim(), to run a simulation under one setting, which can be specified through the following arguments,

  • alpha: scale parameter
  • sigma11: marginal variance for the primary process
  • sigma22: marginal variance for the auxiliary process
  • rho: correlation coefficient
  • n: sample size
  • R: number of simulation, default to 5e6
  • seed: random seed

In this example, we set the following vector for the level of parameters,

## scale parameter
alpha <- 4
## marginal variance 
sigma11 <- sigma22 <- 1
## correlation coefficient
rho <- 0.2
## sample size
n <- 10

Then, we pass the arguments into the function do_sim(). The default number of simulation is 5e6, and we set random seed as 6789

do_sim(alpha = alpha, sigma11 = sigma11, sigma22 = sigma22,
       rho = rho, n = n, seed = 6789)
#> [1] 0.9828559
# takes around 20 seconds...

The do_sim function will generate a large matrix using Cpp code and implement most steps by matrix multiplications. If we increase the sample size n, the code cannot run in a laptop (unless you have a high-end computer with e.g. 32GB…). Therefore, we provide another option using sapply function to release memory pressure:

  • limited.memory: FALSE (default): use the matrix form (need a large memory) or TRUE: the sapply loop (for limited memory)

By setting limited.memory = TRUE, we could run for example n = 20 in laptops. It will show a progress bar if runing the code in terminal or Rstudio.

do_sim(alpha = alpha, sigma11 = sigma11, sigma22 = sigma22,
       rho = rho, n = 20, seed = 6789, 
       limited.memory = TRUE)
# takes around 2 min ...

We would recommand that you run setting with larger n in a server, for example fastlinux in MFCF, along with specifying limited.memory = FALSE.

Example of Tests

The arguments passing to the main function do_sim() should satisfy the following constraints:

  • alpha: scale parameter > 0
  • sigma11: marginal variance for the primary process > 0
  • sigma22: marginal variance for the auxiliary process > 0
  • rho: correlation coefficient in [-1,1]
  • n: sample size > 0, integer
  • R: number of simulation > 0, integer

All the constraints will be checked in do_sim() function.

We use the unit test framework in R for testing, and our package passes the tests.

Here are some examples,

library(testthat)
test_that("the scale parameter should be positive",{
  expect_error(do_sim(alpha = -2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10, R = 100))
})
#> Test passed 🥇
try(do_sim(alpha = -2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10, R = 100))
#> Error in do_sim(alpha = -2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10,  : 
#>   is.numeric(alpha) & alpha > 0 is not TRUE

test_that("the number of simulation should be an integer",{
  expect_error(do_sim(alpha = 2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10, R = 5.5))
})
#> Test passed 😸
try(do_sim(alpha = 2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10, R = 5.5))
#> Error in do_sim(alpha = 2, sigma11 = 1, sigma22 = 1, rho = 0.2, n = 10,  : 
#>   R%%1 == 0 is not TRUE

Reproduce main results in the final report

For the whole simulation in the final report, we provide a function simulation(), which accept a list of parameters, and all the combinations will be passed to do_sim().

For example, in our simulation study, we choose the following parameters for the scale parameter alpha, marginal variance sigma11, sigma22, correlation coefficient rho and sample size n:

alpha.list = c(2,4,8) # scale parameter 
sigma11.list = c(1) # marginal variance
sigma22.list = c(1) # marginal variance
rho.list = c(0.2, 0.5) # correlation coefficient
n.list = c(10,20,30,40,50,60,100) # sample size
R = 5e6 # number of simulation

We can run the following code in a server (e.g. fastlinux in MFCF),

## takes 30 mins in MFCF cpu150 using a single core. 
main.results <- simulation(alpha.list, sigma11.list, sigma22.list, rho.list,
                           n.list, R, seed = 6789)

It will take 30 minutes in MFCF cpu150.

We would recommend that you run the do_sim() function introduced in the previous section to tese the package.

In this vignette, we will not run the code but load the results instead. The results can be found in the repos.

load("results/main_results.RData")

The return of the simulation function is an S3 class, which is named as simkriging_main. We can check it by the base function class() in R,

class(main.results)
#> [1] "simkriging_main"

The following functions for generating tables and figures are all designed as S3 methods for this specific simtrim class.

Monte Carlo Standrad Error (MCSE) in the final report

There is no closed form for the Monte Carlo standard error (MCSE) of relative prediction efficiency in Morris et al. (2019). Therefore, we will repeat the simulation study 300 times under different random seeds to estimate the MCSE.

In the simulation function, we could estimate MCSE by setting estimate.MCSE = TRUE, and the number of repetition is specified by rep.MCSE, for example rep.MCSE = 300 for 300 times of repetition. We could also set ncores greater than 1 to run the code parallelly.

## Repeat the simulation 300 times. 
## Do NOT Run it! load(file.path(filepath,"MCSE_results.RData")) instead. 
## It will take at least 2 DAYS in MFCF cpu150 using a multi-core (4 corse, limited by the Memory). 
MCSE.results <- simulation(alpha.list, sigma11.list, sigma22.list, rho.list,
                           n.list, R, seed = 6789,
                           estimate.MCSE = TRUE, rep.MCSE = 300, 
                           ncores = 4)

In this vignette, we will not run the code but load the results instead. The results can be found in the repos.

load("results/MCSE_results.RData")

The return of the simulation function by setting estimate.MCSE = TRUE is a S3 class named as simkriging_MCSE.

class(MCSE.results)
#> [1] "simkriging_MCSE"

Summarize Results in Tables

Let’s now summarize simulation results in tables. The S3 methods for the simulation results are summary.simkriging_main and summary.simkriging_MCSE for main results and MCSE repectively. We could simply call the summary() function for the returned object from simulation() function. The summary function contains the following argument,

  • object: object of Class ‘simkriging_main’
  • sigma11.select: A vector of the selected sigma11 when drawing the tables. Default to
  • sigma22.select: A vector of the selected sigma22 when drawing the tables. Default to
  • rho.select: A vector of the selected rho when drawing the tables. If missing, all levels of rho will be reported
  • alpha.select: A vector of the selected alpha when drawing the tables. If missing, all levels of alpha will be reported

The return dataframe containing the following columns: + rho: correlation coefficient + alpha: scale parameter + n: sample size + RE: relative prediction efficiency

For example, we choose correlation coefficient rho as 0.2, scale parameter alpha as 4,

summary(main.results, rho.select = 0.2, alpha.select = 4)
rho alpha n RE
0.2 4 10 0.9829
0.2 4 20 0.9809
0.2 4 30 0.9805
0.2 4 40 0.9803
0.2 4 50 0.9803
0.2 4 60 0.9802
0.2 4 100 0.9801

The same function works for MCSE,

summary(MCSE.results, rho.select = 0.2, alpha.select = 4)
rho alpha n mcse
0.2 4 10 0.00011
0.2 4 20 0.00012
0.2 4 30 0.00012
0.2 4 40 0.00012
0.2 4 50 0.00013
0.2 4 60 0.00012
0.2 4 100 0.00011

Summarize Results in Figures

Beside the tables from summary function, we also provide a plot() function to visualize the main simulation results in line plots. Note that the plot function only works for simkriging_main object.

The S3 method for the simulation results is plot.simkriging_main. We could call the plot() function for the returned simkriging_main object from simulation() function.

plot(main.results, rho.select = 0.2)

plot(main.results, rho.select = 0.5)

The figures are same as Fig2 in Zhang and Cai (2015), which indicates that their simulation results are reproducible.

The functions described above are the core functions in SimKriging package. For more information, you could use ? for the name of function to check the manual page.

References

Genton, M. G., and Kleiber, W. (2015), “Cross-covariance functions for multivariate geostatistics,” Statistical Science, 30, 147–163.
Gneiting, T., Kleiber, W., and Schlather, M. (2010), “Matérn cross-covariance functions for multivariate random fields,” Journal of the American Statistical Association, Taylor & Francis, 105, 1167–1177.
Morris, T. P., White, I. R., and Crowther, M. J. (2019), “Using simulation studies to evaluate statistical methods,” Statistics in medicine, Wiley Online Library, 38, 2074–2102.
Zhang, H., and Cai, W. (2015), “When doesn’t cokriging outperform kriging?” Statistical Science, JSTOR, 176–180.
